data.title: AMD: Should Catch A Bid Soon
data.description:
Introduction

I previously wrote on Advanced Micro Devices (NASDAQ:AMD) in February, where I gave AMD a strong buy rating. Since then, the stock has fallen 22%, whilst competitors like NVDA have also fallen ~23% in the same time, signaling a market wide free fall rather than any specific issues with AMD.

In this article, I'll outline why the valuation is getting to a point that is very hard to ignore as a long-term investor, whilst also touching on the reasons to be bullish.

Investment Thesis

A big part of the AMD bull case lies in inference, rather than AI training, which is where NVDA has dominated for the past few years. NVDA no doubt still dominates today with its end to end AI stack with CUDA software, there is going to be a huge amount of demand for a cost-efficient alternative to NVDA's expensive models soon. This is where AMD will slowly, but surely start to win clients from NVDA, though I have no doubt that NVDA will still dominate the vast majority of the market.

My AMD investment thesis does not rely on AMD winning against NVDA. That won't happen. But it does rely on AMD starting to prove they can win in the inference space, which they have already started doing. For example, Oracle (ORCL) has already signed a multi-billion dollar contract with AMD for 30,000 MI355X GPUs.

Essentially, the gold rush in AI is still really only just getting started and there's little chance that all of this can be supplied by just one vendor in NVDA. I've been saying for a while that NVDA's high margin, and complete dominance (almost monopoly) in the space simply isn't sustainable for how vast the market is becoming. The next big players are going to start seeing a lot more opportunities creep up as more and more firms lean into the AI world. If AMD can develop an alternative to NVDA's GPUs which are more cost-effective, and more efficient, the opportunity for them is vast. In the next few months, AMD has the release of their MI350 which will employ chiplet architecture and likely be the big push that AMD needs to start seriously winning share from NVDA.

Risks To Bull Case

The main risk to the bull case above that I see is that:

  1. NVDA will continue to dominate more than I forecast - The pricing power NVDA have over AMD (and the rest of the market) is remarkable (~25bps). As long as NVDA do not become supply constrained, they have complete pricing power in the market, as well as the ability to continue to reinvest in their growth and innovation which they already are. NVDA are investing heavily in industries such as AV's, quantum, robotics etc - the industries of tomorrow. This is all happening whilst AMD is fighting to become the alternative in today's industries.
  2. On top of this, we've also got the dangers of competitors like INTC who have much higher R&D budgets than AMD. AMD has failed to win share in the past few years compared to NVDA simply because they didn't innovate well enough. There's every chance that this continues, and they lose out to the next generation of competitors too.
  3. The final big risk I see is a general macroeconomic risk in tariffs slowing down the global economy and AI CapEx spend. There's also the risk that AMD struggle to onshore their manufacturing at a rate that NVDA can. I don't believe these are specific AMD risks though as much as the first two. Struggles here will cause a slowdown for all semiconductor companies, and not just AMD.

Valuation

AMD is now trading at their lowest earnings multiple in nearly a decade so from a historical basis, this is a clear buy. But I never like to solely look at historical valuations because it doesn't take into account whether the stock was overvalued over the last few years.

Analysts currently estimate $6.2 EPS in FY26, meaning AMD currently trades at 13x FY26 earnings. This means that if AMD only reaches analyst estimates of $6.2 EPS whilst trading at a PE of 20x (same levels as January 2023 PE multiple), then AMD is a $124 stock, which gives a 46% increase from today's price.

For me, a 46% increase is probably not worth the risk of investing in AMD. However, if I look at this a slightly different way in terms of PEG multiples, then I think this valuation paints a slightly different story where the risk to reward is a lot more favorable.

Firstly, AMD currently trades at a PEG of 0.63 which is far below the 1x normal range. If we assume AMD meets analyst estimates of $6.2 in FY26, they will have a 36.8% CAGR over the next 2 years.

At a PEG of 1x, the Price/Earnings multiple is the same as the EPS growth rate so we can assume a PE of 36.8 which would put AMD at $228, or 166% ahead of today's valuation just based on hitting analyst estimates in FY26.

Conclusion

With all this being said, I think the upside potential far outweighs the downside potential for AMD at a PEG of 0.63x and the lowest PE multiple in nearly a decade. With new product launches happening in 2025, increased innovation, and lots of confidence from Lisa Su, the AMD CEO, I think AMD today offers a nice opportunity ahead for investors with a 3-5 year horizon.

data.title: Samsara: Uncertainties Keep Me On The Sidelines
data.description:
Samsara (NYSE:IOT) has fallen significantly since my last publication. Even after the fall I believe that the stock is overvalued because it produces low FCF margins. Additionally, it has high terminal value risk because of the impact of advanced driving automation and ADAS that specifically use cameras for improved driving. Due to the uncertainties, causing a terminal value risk, I rate Samsara a hold.

Background

In my last article about Samsara, I gave a strong opinion about its large P/S combined with low operating and FCF margins. Most high-quality tech companies with high multiples suffered major contractions. Samsara, due to its financial and end market characteristics, was one of the most affected.

Samsara should still be a strong partner for large operation companies that will face lower global growth due to tariffs. Companies will focus more on reducing costs, and Samsara is highly effective in doing so.

Strategy

Streamline, improve and reduce costs on each company's fleet, while creating platformization. Large logistics operators have asset heavy and high labor intensity costs associated with their operations. A slight reduction in the operator cost might translate into a considerable higher profit. Solutions like Samsara’s don’t need to be super advanced and effective to produce small ROI gains. That’s a strong reason for Samsara’s fast growth. Of course, there is competition, and it should only increase with time. To defend its competitive advantage, Samsara is building a comprehensive platform of asset management and optimization with a significant data, feedback and economies of scale advantages.

In the latest earnings call, the company’s management said this:

So some of these newer products, forms is a great example. Asset tags is another one. They're really net new. These are not products that were widely available in the industry. […] That being said, there's competitors for each of these products, but we're really unique in terms of how we structured the whole system as a single platform

Some of the highlights of an IDC study about the benefits of Samsara conclude that the company produces 815% ROI and an average of 2.02 million dollars in fleet benefits per year per company. The fleet benefits include a 6% reduction in overall operating costs, 14% less idle time, 4% savings on fuel, 9% lower maintenance expenses, and a 10% increase in vehicle lifespan.

Samsara complete platform and sky-high ROI should make the company a giant of large-scale operations digitalization and optimization.

Capture a high number of assets under its service and fast. By expanding fast and optimizing for large player deals, IOT is able to absorb more data. Especially data related to safety driving. Whereas route optimization and vehicle preventive maintenance are well-researched and applied in commercial settings, safety driving recommendations and ratings are less researched and there is less data available, opening a large opportunity for Samsara to provide the best safety models. By growing faster, feedback is also more intensive, helping the company implement its system with truck drivers.

There was a growth of 50% in data points and 25% in miles traveled, feeding Samsara safety models, a major differentiation factor for the company.

Financials

Gross margins have consistently been increasing. IOT’s large contract NNR is growing quicker than the small contract one, creating more profitable contracts.

The FCF/EC is high, but most FCF is tied to SBC. There has been a massive increase in this selected metric YoY. Samsara should keep expanding its margins. Consequently, producing higher and higher FCF per unit of employed capital.

There is no, or very little goodwill. All products were built from scratch and are highly effective. Showing a strong R&D company.

The working capital days are negative and are decreasing YoY, which is very positive for the business. There is no working capital invested, which is creating a float that could be invested in other assets, including treasuries.

For now, the analysts are predicting that growth will remain consistent and could even increase in the next three years. Maybe these estimates will change with the increasing impact of tariffs in the global economy. Still, analysts are very positive about Samsara's long-term growth.

Opportunities

Samsara is in the early stages of expansion. A very high number of intense operational businesses are still conducting and managing operations using more simplified approaches and legacy technologies. The ones that wish to surf the wave of increased digitalization should buy from Samsara due to its more advanced and comprehensive operations platform. And it should bring very high results, especially for the larger operations.

In the latest earnings call the company management said this:

First, we're in the early stages of digitizing a massive market. We're generating over $1 billion in ARR from our core vehicle applications alone. Today, less than half of North American commercial vehicles use telematics, and only about 10% use safety products. As our customers prioritize vehicle digitization first, we see a clear path to expanding our core applications into a multi-billion dollar business. More broadly, the market opportunity beyond vehicles is even earlier in digitization. We see an enormous opportunity for sustained growth.

Samsara’s main market is the vehicle digitalization, but its asset tag feature should also enhance its overall portfolio of solutions. However, the product I want to monitor is the safety one. This is the most difficult product to both implement and develop. When it is well implemented it infuses with the day-to-day operations while positively impacting safe driving. The familiarity of the implemented tool and effectiveness should translate to less churn, more differentiation and ROI gains.

We are at the beginning of operations digitalization. Especially in safety driving features that have massive market potential. This market is also very underpenetrated and should be accretive to ARPU, providing a strong growth runaway for revenue and customer lifetime value.

Risks

Now the company sits at a much more attractive P/S, minimizing the risk of multiple compression.

The trend of increased electrification will impact Samsara, because fuel is cheaper. Thus, there should be less opportunities for strong fuel optimization gains, reducing Samsara’s ROI and attractiveness.

Impact of autonomous driving and advanced ADAS is still uncertain on the company.

Valuation

To understand what growth the market is pricing, I like to do a reverse discounted cash flow analysis. To model the discount rate, I use 10%. To be conservative, I assumed an initial FCF margin of 8%. Then it expands 2% every year. To generate the terminal multiple, I’m using a terminal growth rate of 4.7%. The terminal growth rate is obtained summing a real GDP growth rate of 2.5% and an inflation rate (since 2000) of 2.2%, resulting in the number 4.7%. I also take into consideration the share count to calculate the FCF per share. It expands by 3% every year. With the inputs, the growth is balanced to obtain the current share price.

The model outputs an initial growth of 27.8% that reduces 5% every year, reaching 17.5% in 2034. Considering the sell-side analysts' expectations, Samsara is still overvalued. Despite this, Samsara has a high-quality operation, that should be very hard to buy near fair value.

Conclusion

Samsara is a great business. It provides a very strong ROI for its clients, works with some of the largest logistics operators and has a long growth runaway. I like the data-driven advantage they are building. I would like to see its safety product have more penetration, which in theory should translate into significantly superior Customer Lifetime Value. On the negative side, I can’t predict what will happen with the increased development of advanced ADAS and autonomous driving in vehicles. This should impact Samsara business while creating terminal value risks. I rate Samsara a hold.

data.title: Canadian Banks Ranking 2025 - Which Bank To Hold?
data.description:
Let’s rank the Big 6 Canadian banks from worst to best!

Whether you already hold one of these financial giants or are considering adding a bank to your portfolio, this will help you understand key strengths, weaknesses, and long-term investment potential.

Here’s the good, the bad, and the boring.

Scotiabank (BNS)

  • Bear Case: Volatility from international exposure, no dividend increase in 2024 (effectively a passive cut), and the highest payout ratio among peers. Dead last on most key metrics.
  • Mike’s Take: Better long-term opportunities exist. Unless you see a major turnaround, capital might be better placed elsewhere.

CIBC (CM)

  • Bull Case: Strong performance in 2024, solid focus on domestic retail and commercial banking.
  • Bear Case: Limited growth potential, heavy exposure to the Canadian economy, and high concentration of uninsured mortgages.
  • Mike’s Take: Not a bad bank, just others with stronger positioning.

TD Bank (TD)

  • Bull Case: Large U.S. exposure, strong capital position, potential growth from asset recycling.
  • Bear Case: Impact of the money laundering investigation, capped U.S. growth, and some uncertainty around regulatory resolution.
  • Mike’s Take: A deluxe bond with decent yield and modest growth. Good, but overshadowed by stronger peers.

Bank of Montreal (BMO)

  • Bull Case: Aggressive business model, strong U.S. presence, pioneer in private wealth services.
  • Bear Case: Higher volatility and integration risks from acquiring the Bank of the West.
  • Mike’s Take: Upgraded due to TD’s issues. Great option with upside potential.

National Bank (NA:CA)

  • Bull Case: Top long-term performer with strong growth strategy, smart acquisitions (e.g., Canadian Western Bank), solid wealth, and capital markets business.
  • Bear Case: Still the smallest of the big six, concentrated exposure in Quebec, more vulnerable in a localized recession.
  • Mike’s Take: Still a top personal holding. Temporarily behind RBC due to macro uncertainty.

Royal Bank (RY)

  • Bull Case: Huge diversification, balanced revenue mix (banking, wealth, insurance), robust dividend triangle, steady performance.
  • Bear Case: Large mortgage loan book, highly exposed to the Canadian economy.
  • Mike’s Take: Best all-around performer right now. Strong foundation with size, scale, and stability.

Final Thoughts

  • Don’t collect all six banks like Pokémon.
  • Instead, choose your favorite two based on business model and metrics.
  • Diversify across the financial sector: Consider EQ Bank, goeasy (GSY:CA), Propel Holdings (OTCPK:PRLPF)(PRL:CA), TMX Group (OTCPK:TMXXF)(X:CA), Brookfield (BAM), Intact Financial (OTCPK:IFCZF)(IFC:CA), and life insurers like Manulife (MFC)(MFC:CA), Sun Life (SLF)(SLF:CA), and Great-West Life (OTCPK:GWLIF)(GWO:CA).

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Goldman Sachs: High-Caliber Financial Powerhouse Trading At A Discount
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Goldman Sachs (NYSE:GS) is currently trading at a P/E ratio of 10.51x. Despite this low P/E ratio, the company has maintained a strong historical growth rate that, I think, will continue into the future, and the market could reprice the stock, which could mean a good period of outperformance from the stock while this happens. The company also has a strong brand value given its long track record, and it allows them to maintain market dominance. In this article, I am going to write about why the stock is undervalued and why it could be a great investment for the long term.

Introduction

Goldman Sachs was founded in the year 1869 and is currently based in New York City. The company primarily engages in various Investment Banking services and has multiple arms, such as trading and asset management. This allows the company to have a diversified income stream. This allows them to maintain stability and consistently reward their shareholders. Their investment banking segment is still their largest by revenue, but other segments are growing faster and are expected to further contribute to their growth. So let's get started with the Growth estimates section.

Growth potential

Over the last 10 years, their net income has grown at an annualised rate of 5.35%. This growth was primarily driven by a strong economy and increased growth from their asset management segment. Currently, the investment banking sector is expected to grow anywhere between 7% to 9% in the coming decade, according to various sources. This growth is primarily driven by the increasing financialisation of the economy

However, I tend to believe that these growth estimates are too optimistic, and if we do see a recession in the coming years, this could be way slower. Currently, due to Trump tariffs, the odds of a recession are increasing, and this could be a setback that could massively slow growth, and earnings could even decline for a couple of years. Also, as we can see from the image below, Seeking Alpha expects the Earnings growth rate over the next 5 years to be in the 11% range. This indicates that the potential for growth over the next few years is strong.

So, despite these optimistic projections, just to be extremely conservative, I have taken the growth rate over the next few years to be close to around 3% annually. This is also in line with the long-term average US economic growth rate. I am going to use this growth expectation for valuation purposes later in the article. But before that, let me analyse the recession risk the stock faces and its potential impact.

Recession Risk

As of recently, the recession risk is increasing according to Polymarket, we have a 66% chance of recession within this year. So I think it would be safe to do scenario analysis to see how a potential recession impacts earnings. I think it's best to look back at the last major recession in 2008 and see how a potential recession could play out. During 2008, the company's earnings declined from a peak of 11.6 billion dollars to a low of around 2.3 billion. That is despite the fact that the recession primarily impacted investment banks, with one of its biggest competitors at it time, Lehman Brothers, going bust, and the Investment banking sector was the hardest hit sector; despite that, the company managed to maintain profitability. This indicates the company's strong ability to manage risk.

Stronger Risk Management:- After the global financial crisis, many banks, including Goldman Sachs, started to implement advanced risk monitoring by performing regular quarterly assessments. These assessments regularly monitor and perform stress tests to ensure stability in all scenarios. Apart from these, the company also implemented the BASEL 3 framework and Dodd-Frank Act, which further mandated the company to strengthen its balance sheet.

Lowered leverage:- In 2007, the company and a leverage ratio of over 26x; this ratio is currently much lower at close to 13x. This indicates massive deleveraging and indicates that the company is much more risk-averse today than it was in 2008. Any recession could still impact earnings, but any risk of a complete blow-up is much lower and not an issue.

How would earnings do in a recession?

In a recession, like any other recession, earnings would take a hit, but I expect any hit in earnings to be temporary and for earnings to revert to normal levels as the economy recovers. If the economic recovery were to be slower than expected, then the market would also take time to recover. So I expect the company to outperform the market in a recession recovery. The company's improved risk management and lower leverage should help it recover much faster than it did in 2008.

Valuation

As we can see from the image below, last year, the company returned close to $15 billion to its shareholders. This adds up to almost $47 per share in capital returned to shareholders. I have assumed this return of capital shrinks to about $40 per share starting next year. I am going to use this 40 as a baseline EPS for the DCF analysis further below.

As mentioned previously, I have used the earnings of $40 per share, and I have also taken a growth rate of 3% for the foreseeable future, as I wrote about in the growth section. I have also kept the terminal growth rate of 3%, which is in line with the long-term US economic growth rate. I have also decided to use an extremely conservative discount rate of 12%, as the long-term return of the S&P 500 is 10%, and I have added an additional 2% to account for the additional risk that the financial sector poses. By putting all the variables in a DCF calculator, we get an intrinsic value per share of $457. This indicates that after the recent correction, the stock is fairly priced. I also think that a discount rate of 12% and a growth rate of 3% is very conservative and should account for any major risks in the stock.

Q4 Analysis And Further Outlook

Goldman Sachs reported Q4 earnings on January 15th 2025. The company reported net income of $3.92 billion, up 22% from the same time last year. The company also issued a full-year EPS projection of $45.78 for 2025. This is expected to be an increase of 12.9% from the previous year's Earnings. Also, according to Wall Street, in the fiscal year 2026, the adjusted EPS is expected to grow 12.2% year-over-year to $51.34. These growth estimates are much higher than the 3% I assumed, but this makes sure the assumptions I made were very conservative. If these projections by the analysts were to come true, this could be a positive and add further upside to the stock. The company also brought back $3.5 billion of stock in the last quarter. These buybacks increase the shareholders' share in the company and work to increase EPS further. Now moving on to the Risks section.

Risks

As I have already covered the recession risk in the previous segment, I will briefly touch on the other major risks that the company could face and that would majorly impact shareholders.

Legal and Regulatory Risk: As global financial institutions, such as Goldman Sachs, operate under extensive regulatory scrutiny. Changes in laws, regulations, or enforcement practices can lead to increased compliance costs, restrictions on operations, or legal proceedings, all of which can negatively affect profitability. I do think this risk is small, as the financial sector is already over-regulated, and any further regulations are unlikely.

Strategic Risk: This involves risks arising from adverse business decisions or the failure to implement appropriate strategies to achieve organizational goals. The Strategic Risk and Stress Testing team at Goldman Sachs is tasked with developing and executing the firm's strategic risk capabilities, ensuring that business strategies align with the firm's risk appetite and regulatory expectations. This all but guarantees that strategic risk is small, and Goldman could easily catch up if it happens to fall behind.

What Type Of Investors Would This Be A Good Fit For?

I think this would be a right fit for any investor looking to gain exposure to the financial sector, and think they are already underexposed to this sector and want a stable, long-term player with a long proven track record, it would be a great fit for them. It would also be a great fit for any investor looking to add a potentially undervalued stock to their portfolio would find Goldman Sachs as an attractive investment. It would also be a great fit for dividend growth investors due to the company's strong dividend track record. Given the valuation, it would be a great fit for many. However, I think people who are already overexposed to the financial sector should avoid it.

Conclusion

Goldman Sachs represents a compelling long-term investment opportunity. Trading at a low P/E ratio of 10.51x, the stock appears undervalued despite the firm's consistent historical growth. Its diversified revenue streams-from investment banking to asset management-and strong risk management practices contribute to its solid financial foundation. Conservative growth assumptions of 3% may understate its true potential, given favorable market conditions and strategic initiatives, including share buybacks. For investors seeking stable exposure in the financial sector with significant capital appreciation potential, Goldman Sachs stands out as an attractive and resilient choice, poised to benefit from future market developments and evolving industry dynamics.

data.title: CEF Insights: Navigating Senior Loans For Income
data.description:
CEFA:

Welcome to CEF Insights, your source for closed-end fund and interval fund education and insights, brought to you by the Closed-End Fund Association.

Today we are joined by Scott Caraher, Head of Senior Loans with Nuveen Asset Management and Portfolio Manager for Nuveen Credit Strategies Income Fund, symbol, NYSE:JQC.

Scott, we're happy to have you with us today.

Scott Caraher:

Thanks so much for having me. I'm happy to be here.

CEFA:

Scott, can you start by providing a brief overview of your team and how the leveraged finance team fits within the broader Nuveen organization?

Scott Caraher:

Yes, so our leveraged finance team, which is a team of approximately 40 individuals comprised of portfolio managers, research analysts and traders, we manage approximately $45 billion, and we sit within the broader Nuveen global fixed income platform, which manages approximately $600 billion. Our leveraged finance team, we have fully integrated, and we look across high-yield bonds, senior loans, as well as CLOs and structured credit.

CEFA:

I mentioned the Nuveen Credit Strategies Income Fund, one of two senior loan-focused closed-in funds you manage. Can you discuss the investment strategy as well as the key objectives of the fund?

Scott Caraher:

Yeah, so the primary objective of the fund is income and yield for our investors with a secondary goal of capital appreciation, and we really achieve this through a number of different ways. As I mentioned, our leveraged finance team, we're looking at the senior loan market, the high-yield market, as well as structured credit, and all of these asset classes are available as tools to be used as investment opportunities within JQC.

Now, the primary asset allocation within the fund is normally going to be loans, but we do have a broad level of flexibility to move those asset allocations around between those three, loans, high-yield bonds, and structured credit when we see more attractive relative value across those three asset classes.

CEFA:

How do you and your team evaluate the broad range of senior loans to make specific security selections? And how do you address risk management within the portfolio?

Scott Caraher:

So the team, we're looking at the entire leveraged loan market. So, the leveraged loan market is an approximately trillion and a half dollar market. Our team, while we look at the entire market, we are focused on the larger, more liquid part of the market, and so that really comprises approximately $900 billion of the $1.5 trillion market. The reason why we're focused on that part of the market is we want to really focus on the larger, more liquid parts of the market, and the reason for that is liquidity is incredibly important to us and the most liquid loans in the market, these loans usually trade every single day.

That's incredibly important, because the second part of your question about risk management, risk management is something that we do and that we focus on every single day. It's the most important thing we do. Our day starts every morning with the morning meeting where we're talking about the risks and opportunities that we're seeing that day. Our day concludes in the afternoon with an investment committee where we're sitting around and doing essentially the same thing, looking for opportunities, but also talking about where there are potential risks in the overall economy and how those are filtering through to the portfolio. So by focusing on the largest most liquid part of the market, it allows us to risk manage every single day.

One of the nice things about the large team that we have, over 40 individuals focused on these markets, is our team of analysts every single day we're talking to companies, we're talking to research analysts, we're doing the work to really understand what's going on both at the overall economic level, but at the industry, sub-industry and individual company level. When we bring all of those data points together, it really allows us to both stay on top of, as well as stay ahead of risks that we might see.

Going back to liquidity, when we see or are concerned that there are risks, we have the ability through the liquidity in the portfolio to actively risk manage and sell those loans we think potentially pose a risk to the portfolio.

CEFA:

Are there any characteristics unique to senior loans that provide a degree of diversification to an income portfolio that investors may not get from treasuries or corporates?

Scott Caraher:

Absolutely. This is one of the things that we've really tried to focus on educating investors about. Loans are a very unique and powerful asset class, and they are really the only liquid fixed income asset class that has no duration. The duration on a loan is essentially zero, and when an investor thinks about their fixed income portfolio, there's a lot of embedded duration in that fixed income portfolio. So by investing in loans, you are by definition bringing down the duration of your fixed income portfolio.

Never has that been more evident and more clear than over the last few years, because as the Fed has raised rates to combat inflation, the duration portion of investors' fixed income portfolios has in many cases caused losses and caused some issues. Really over the last I'd say 20 years, investors have looked at loans as a tactical allocation within their overall fixed income portfolios.

What I mean by that is they think about allocating to loans when rates are rising and reducing their allocation when the Fed is cutting rates. It really has not worked broadly speaking for investors. I think we all know that timing markets is next to impossible, and so what we've really advocated for, and I think what the last few years has really shown is that loans should be a permanent strategic allocation in investors' overall fixed income portfolio.

Because what that does is it brings down your duration, and it increases the yield or income in your fixed income portfolio, because the one thing that you're taking, you're taking some level of credit risk when you allocate to loans, you're not taking duration risk, but you are taking credit risk. But that can be balanced within that overall fixed income allocation, and so loans can be a very powerful and permanent allocation within an investor's fixed income portfolio.

CEFA:

Scott, how can an allocation to a strategy focused on senior loans like JQC help fixed income investors enhance returns and reduce volatility?

Scott Caraher:

So like we talked about in the last question, the loans by definition are going to reduce volatility caused by duration. You've seen that over the last few years as the duration within overall fixed income portfolios has caused volatility. Loans are really the best performing liquid fixed income asset class since the Fed started raising rates. The loan market has provided very solid returns since the Fed has started raising rates, and that has been very beneficial to an overall fixed income portfolio.

If you think about where we sit today, the Fed is on pause, but they're on pause at a very high-level of short-term rates. So today the yield on the loan index is just over 8%, and so you're getting very significant amounts of income in yield by owning or having an allocation to loans that you're not going to get in certain other longer duration fixed income asset classes. So you're getting more yield and you're getting significantly less duration, and so when you combine those two things, they do help reduce volatility as well as enhance returns.

CEFA:

Scott, we've seen expectations for Federal Reserve rate cuts shift a number of times in the last few months, we have also witnessed increased volatility in markets. Where do you see the fixed income markets currently and what is Nuveen's outlook for 2025?

Scott Caraher:

So answering that part first, what we see, what we believe at Nuveen is that the Fed is going to cut rates twice this year, likely in the back half of the year. Now, those cuts are going to start from a very high base, right? The Fed has raised rates a lot over the last few years to combat inflation and bring inflation down, and that inflation fight has not fully been won yet. But we do think the Fed will cut rates twice this year, that will bring down loan yields on the margin.

But where we see opportunities today, there's a significant amount of opportunity in the loan market. Now, we are absolutely in a credit picker's market, though. High rates for an extended period of time, as well as high inflation, has caused dispersion in the loan market. So if you drill down into the components of the loan market, you have some very good quality loans that trade or offer yields anywhere between 6.5% and 7.5%. As you go out the risk spectrum, there's more yield available in the loan market.

What we think is interesting on a very selective basis are loans that are trading at discounts as a result of the inflation and high rates that we've seen over the last few years. If you look at the $1.5 trillion loan market, approximately $160 billion of that $1.5 trillion market trades between 85 and 95 cents on the dollar with an average yield of approximately 12%.

Now, this cohort of the market cannot be bought just blindly. We are absolutely in a credit picking environment where if you have the ability and the skill set and the resources to really go in and understand and find that subset of that $160 billion of loans that our credit improving stories are going to be impacted by or benefit from rates coming down, inflation coming down. Some of these loans potentially have the ability to be impacted in a positive way by the new administration.

There are pockets of value that we see, and we think we're going to continue to see, and so we're in a very interesting market where you can take what we believe to be very little credit risk and earn 6.5% to 7.5%, and then take very targeted credit risk and earn anywhere from 7.5% to 12.5%. So we're incredibly excited and intrigued by the market setup that we're in, and we think that this is going to continue for the next year or two, and we think there's a lot of opportunity to be had for investors.

CEFA:

What are the most significant risks in the current environment?

Scott Caraher:

So the biggest risk when thinking about the loan market is credit risk and economic risk, and this is a market that is not investment grade, so these loans trade or are rated by the rating agencies below investment grade. So, that is the biggest risk you're taking by allocating to the loan market is credit risk. As we think about risk broadly speaking to the market, we need to think about the economy. If the economy were to continue to slow, if we ended up in a recession, that would certainly not be good for the loan market.

Now, we've been through plenty of recessions before over the last 25 years. Generally speaking, it does create some level of volatility for the lowest quality part of the market, but the vast majority of the loan market holds up just fine. The average default rate in the loan market going back 20+ years is approximately 3%, which is very reasonable, and the loan market can withstand a recession like it has many times over the last 20+ years.

CEFA:

Within the senior loan sector, are you finding valuations attractive?

Scott Caraher:

Absolutely, but again, it is a credit picker's market. We believe you cannot just buy the loan market blindly. Between inflation, higher interest rates, a number of lower rated issuers, and also what's going on in Washington with the new administration clearly focused on cutting government spending, you need to be very careful as to where you're allocating capital, what loans you are buying.

But broadly speaking, when you think about loans yielding, really good quality loans with what we believe to be very little chance of default yielding between 6.5% to 7.5% and a large cohort of the market yielding north of that between 7.5% and 12.5%, we see a lot of value, but again, you need to know where to go to look to find that value.

CEFA:

Where are you seeing the best opportunities to put new capital to work?

Scott Caraher:

So with new capital, again, going back to kind of that barbell approach, right? So new capital would be allocated selectively to better quality issuers, but at the same time, what I believe right now, one of the best opportunities to allocate new capital is to that cohort of the market that's trading between 85 and 95 cents on the dollar that's yielding approximately 12%, but you need to be very patient, you need to be very diligent in assessing when and where to allocate new capital.

So that's where we're focusing a lot of our time is trying to identify those opportunities, but again, you can't blindly allocate to that part of the market, but if you're diligent and patient, you are going to be able and can find some very interesting opportunities both today, and we believe that we're going to continue to find those opportunities as we move forward.

CEFA:

Scott, the fund is conducting a secondary offering to raise new capital to take advantage of the opportunities you just outlined. What are the terms of the offering?

Scott Caraher:

So, JQC is conducting a transferable rights offering that will allow investors the opportunity to buy additional shares of the fund at a discount to its market price. Shareholders of record as of March 31st will receive one right for each share owned, and five rights are required to subscribe for a new share.

The offer expires on April 29 and the subscription price will be the higher of 95% of the average market price over the final five days of the offer, or 90% of the NAV at the close of the market on April 29. More information about the offer can be found at nuveen.com/rights.

CEFA:

Scott, thank you so much for taking the time to join us today.

Scott Caraher:

Thanks for having me.

CEFA:

And thank you for joining us for another CEF Insights podcast.

data.title: Top REITs To Take On Trump Tariffs: Average Yield Over 5.5%
data.description:
Trump's Tariff Tennis Continues

As Donald Trump's tariff tennis match drags on, Wall Street is getting whiplash trying to keep up. Equities rallied on Wednesday after President Trump said he would freeze the implementation of his sweeping reciprocal tariffs for the next 90 days as deal talks with trading partners play out. He did not back down on China, however, announcing that the U.S. would instead raise tariffs on exports from the nation to 125%. The S&P 500 rallied more than 9% in afternoon trading, while the Nasdaq Composite added nearly 12%. The Dow Jones Industrial Average climbed 7.5%.

Amid the wild swings in U.S. equities, a Treasury auction of 10-year bonds attracted solid demand, helping curb a deepening sell-off in the bond market and calming fears that investors are fleeing U.S. debt. Still, the 10-year US Treasury (US10Y) yield remains higher than usual for a market pullback, jumped as high as 4.51% before retreating to 4.41% on Wednesday, while the 30-year yield (US30Y) briefly rose above 5% before lowering to 4.9%. (See how all Treasury yields are performing on Seeking Alpha's market data page.)

Treasury Yields by Time Length (as of 4/8/25)

During volatility, treasury yields—which move inversely to prices—would be expected to retreat in response to mounting recession fears. But, tariffs have triggered concerns about sticky inflation, thus driving yields upwards. One reason for the peculiar sell-off in bonds could be connected to the unwinding of the "basis trade," according to Apollo Asset Management. A basis trade involves betting the price difference between a Treasury bond and its corresponding Treasury futures contract.

U.S. Treasury Yields Surged on Tariff Turmoil Earlier This Week

Signs of stress in the bond market are the last thing you want to see during a volatile period for equities. The situation is uncomfortably reminiscent of the COVID-19 crisis, when skyrocketing Treasury yields triggered a global "dash-for-cash" by investors, forcing the Federal Reserve to step in with massive bond purchases.

In a perfect world, the equities rally will continue, and Treasury yields will return to normal levels. But a 90-day pause on tariffs does little to guarantee that the worst of the volatility is behind us. Since markets began to rally on Wednesday afternoon, Fed funds futures pricing now reflects only a 17.6% chance that the central bank will intervene and lower rates at its May meeting-down from 41.5% earlier that morning, according to the CME FedWatch tool. As I noted in Top AI Stocks, Fed chair Powell signaled earlier this month that the central bank was hesitant to intervene until the fallout from tariffs becomes clearer. Trump's three-month tariff freeze suggests it will remain on the sidelines for the near future.

Futures Traders Predict an 82.4% Chance Rates Will Stay the Same

You don't need to wait 90 days to see how tariffs will play out in the market. There are actions you can take to hedge your portfolio against volatility today. As the safety of bonds comes into question, one safe-haven asset class continues to demonstrate stability in the ongoing trade war: real estate investment trusts (REITs).

REITs: A Port in the Storm

REITs can be a defensive haven during periods of turbulence due to their stable dividend income, tangible asset backing, and inflation-hedging properties. Their required high dividend payouts provide consistent cash flow, while real estate holdings help preserve value. Specific sectors, like healthcare and residential REITs, offer steady demand, making them less vulnerable to economic downturns. Additionally, REITs often correlate less to equities, enhancing portfolio diversification. Though sensitive to interest rates, their ability to adjust rents with inflation and generate passive income adds an extra layer of resilience.

In my latest webinar, Investing During a Correction, I discussed the importance of maintaining a barbell approach in tumultuous markets. Combining fundamentally strong stocks with income-generating dividend stocks can provide a steady income stream and mitigate risk. When it comes to the first part of that strategy, Seeking Alpha's Quant System can help identify Strong Buy-rated stocks that are well-positioned for strong returns once conditions stabilize (see my latest recommendations on Top AI Stocks and 'Biggest Loser' stocks primed for a comeback.)

As for the second part, investors should prioritize capital preservation by focusing on dividend-paying stocks with strong fundamentals. REITs can offer solid yields and strong dividend safety, two critical investment characteristics in a market sell-off. They also tend to get a boost from lower interest rates, making them even more attractive when/if the Fed finally opts to get involved.

Top 5 REITs to Take on Trump Tariffs

Using SA's Quant System, I've selected five high-quality, diversified REITs to help solidify the income-generating side of your barbell approach, offering above-average yields, solid dividend safety grades, and solid AFFO and FFO growth. With an average dividend yield of nearly 5%, these REITs make great options for income-oriented investors and growth investors seeking more stability in the current environment.

REITs for Tariff Uncertainty: Yielding 5.8% on Average (as of 4/9/25)

How We Scan for Top REITs

To screen for the best REITs on the market, SA Quant analyzes the full real estate investment universe using powerful computer processing and our special REIT 'Quantamental' analysis. It then ranks the REITs with the strongest collective value, growth, profitability, risk, and price momentum metrics vs. the broader REIT sector as well as multiples and payout ratios specific to REITs, including FFO, AFFO, FAD, Gross Properties, and more. Over the past 6 years, the backtested strategy has delivered impressive returns, more than doubling the Real Estate Select Sector SPDR ETF (XLRE) as of April 8, 2025.

Seeking Alpha's REIT Recommendations Eclipse the Real Estate Sector

From these results, I've identified five stocks that exhibit excellent income-generating characteristics in addition to the following criteria:

  1. Quant Rating: Strong Buy

  2. Market capitalization > 500M

  3. Stock price >$2.00

  4. Solid dividend yield

  5. Positive factor grades across the board

The stocks below represent an attractive blend of income-yielding characteristics, as well as other positive SA Quant Factor Grades and Quant Dividend Grades. To see all of the 'Strong Buy' recommendations, go to the Top REITs Quant Screener.

1. Alexander & Baldwin, Inc. (ALEX)

  • Market Capitalization: $1.2B

  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy

  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of 4/9/25): 3 out of 173

  • Quant Industry Ranking (as of 4/9/25): 1 out of 14

  • Dividend Yield (FWD): 5.56%

  • Dividend Safety Grade: B-

Ranked first among Diversified REITs and 5th in the Real Estate sector overall, ALEX is a diversified REIT based in Honolulu, Hawaii. It focuses on properties in its home state, ranging from retail, office centers, industrial assets, and 142 acres of ground leases. While it has shed around 12% over the past month amid widespread market turmoil, its strong financial performance and excellent Quant Factor and Dividend grades leave it primed for a steady potential recovery.

The 153-year-old island REIT reported fourth-quarter FFO of $0.30 per share, a $0.03 year-over-year increase, and AFFO of $0.19 per share, a $0.02 increase-improvements fueled primarily by stronger performance by its commercial real estate portfolio. It is currently in the process of monetizing its remaining non-core assets, efforts that were reflected by an increase in Q4 operating profits to 22M and an FFO to gross margin (TTM) ratio of 91%, a 42% difference from the sector.

As Seeking Alpha analyst Gen Alpha recently wrote:

ALEX's strategy includes monetizing non-core landholdings and reinvesting in industrial development, supported by a low leverage ratio and robust leasing activity. It demonstrates solid FFO growth, high occupancy rates, and a well-covered dividend, making it a compelling choice for income investors.

ALEX Stock Quant Breakdown

ALEX's 5.56% dividend yield is backed by a 77% payout ratio, a 5-year CAGR of 4%, and forward AFFO growth of nearly 8%, a 180% difference from the 2.75% sector median.

ALEX Dividend Scorecard (as of 4/9/25)

The Hawaiian REIT also exhibits solid dividend safety characteristics, including an FFO interest rate coverage ratio of 5.24, an 81% difference vs. the sector's 2.9. Analysts have expressed confidence in its high forward dividend yield, with a 5.5% consensus ratio for December 2026. Dividend growth is also attractive, with forward AFFO growth of 7.9%, a 180% difference from the sector.

Like ALEX, my next pick combines solid financials with high dividend growth and safety grades to help shield portfolios from tariff trauma.

2. CareTrust REIT, Inc. (CTRE)

  • Market Capitalization: $5B

  • Sector: REITs

  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy

  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of 4/9/25): 5 out of 173

  • Quant Industry Ranking (as of 4/9/25): 1 out of 17

  • Dividend Yield (FWD): 4.7%

  • Dividend Safety Grade: A+

Currently, the top Quant-ranked Healthcare REIT and included within my top dividend stocks for 2025 (listen to me discuss these picks on SA's Investing Experts podcast here), CareTrust REIT primarily invests in skilled nursing facilities and senior housing properties across the U.S. Returning 8% over the past year up nearly 1% over the past month, CTRE has remained fairly insulated from volatility thanks to solid fundamentals and strong tailwinds across the senior care field compared to the overall market.

CTRE demonstrated excellent Q4 2024 results, with year-over-year FFO per share growth of 11.1% to $0.40. The REIT made $1.5B in of acquisitions in 2025 at a high stabilized yield of 9.7%. As Gen Alpha noted in March on its latest earnings results:

CTRE's forward acquisition strategy is supported by a very strong balance sheet that is by design intended to fund robust acquisitions. This includes an ultra-low net debt-to-EBITDA ratio of 0.5x, sitting far below the 6.0x level generally considered safe for REITs.

CTRE Stock Quant Breakdown

CTRE'S 4.7% forward dividend yield is supported by a 68.8% payout ratio, a 5-year CAGR of 5.4%, and forward AFFO growth of nearly 10%, a 254% difference from the 2.8% sector median.

CTRE Dividend Scorecard (as of 4/9/25)

The healthcare REIT also boasts with 'A+' forward FFO growth of 11.6%, a 334.8% premium to the 2.7% sector median, and an AFFO/total revenue margin (TTM) of 96%, a 144.8% difference to the sector median. It also boasts an attractive valuation, its forward P/E of 16.55, a near 44% discount to the sector despite above-average momentum. Notably, CTRE has an 'A+' dividend safety grade, supported by an FFO interest coverage ratio of 6.34x, making it a reliable option for above-average yield in Healthcare REITs.

Next up is another REIT with excellent dividend safety characteristics.

3. VICI Properties Inc. (VICI)

  • Market Capitalization: $31B

  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy

  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of 4/9/25): 6 out of 173

  • Quant Industry Ranking (as of 4/9/25): 1 out of 11

  • Dividend Yield (FWD): 5.88%

  • Dividend Safety Grade: B+

One of my top 3 high-yielding dividend stocks for 2025, VICI Properties, counts Caesars Palace and MGM Grand among its portfolio of Vegas trophy assets and other commercial property assets. Gaming sector REITs boast long lease terms and above-average rent escalators, as reflected by VICI's 100% occupancy rate and highly profitable triple-net lease model. The company has remained flat YTD, signaling a relatively safe bet in tumultuous markets. Despite missing Wall Street expectations with its Q4 FFO estimates, VICI has demonstrated solid revenue growth and annual AFFO per share growth of 5.1% to $0.57 in Q4 2024 from $0.55 in Q4 2023.

VICI Stock Quant Breakdown

VICI's impressive 5.88% dividend yield is backed by a 64.4% payout ratio, a 5-year CAGR of 7.7%, and six years of consecutive dividend growth-a 2,045.8% difference to the sector reflected by its 'A-' Dividend Consistency Grade.

VICI Dividend Scorecard (as of 4/9/25)

Analysts appear convinced that its forward dividend yield will only continue to improve, with a consensus estimate of 6.2% for December 2026. VICI also comes at an attractive price tag, as shown by a forward P/AFFO of 12.6 versus the sector's 13.5, a 6.7% discount, and a forward P/FFO of 10.8, a more than 8% discount.

A global trade war is not a time to make risky bets. With its solid dividend yield and strong safety characteristics, VICI is a REIT worth rolling the dice on.

4. Regency Centers Corporation (REG)

  • Market Capitalization: $11.9B

  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy

  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of 4/9/25): 9 out of 173

  • Quant Industry Ranking (as of 4/9/25): 1 out of 23

  • Dividend Yield (FWD): 4.3%

  • Dividend Safety Grade: B

The top Quant-ranked Retail REIT, Regency Centers Corporation, owns over 470 retail properties across the U.S. including top grocery stores such as Whole Foods, Publix, Safeway, and Trader Joe's. Returning nearly 8% over the past year and remaining flat, REG is weathering the stock market storm with a strong balance sheet and resilient, geographically diversified business model, prioritizing high-quality supermarket chains in affluent areas. Its 4.3% dividend yield offers dependable income and modest growth, backed by strong Quant Dividend grades.

REG Stock Quant Breakdown

While REG may be a little more expensive with an overall 'D+' Valuation Grade, its modest dividend yield is well-insulated by its payout ratio of 62.5% and a 3.2% five-year growth rate. Perhaps its biggest selling point is its 'A' Dividend Growth Grade, boosted by an 'A+' dividend growth rate (TTM) of 31%-an impressive 1,854.57% difference from the 1.6% sector median.

REG Dividend Scorecard (as of 4/9/25)

FFO growth of 4.5%, a 67.2% difference from the sector, and AFFO growth of 4%, a 42% difference, highlight REG's potential as a long-term investment. Analysts are bullish that REG's dividend will remain stable, with a consensus estimate of 4.46% for December 2026.

My next selection also packs strong Quant Dividend Grades across the board, while boasting the highest forward dividend yield on this list.

5. EPR Properties (EPR)

  • Market Capitalization: $3.3B

  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy

  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of 4/9/25): 14 out of 173

  • Quant Industry Ranking (as of 4/9/25): 2 out of 11

  • Dividend Yield (FWD): 8.13%

  • Dividend Safety Grade: B-

EPR Properties is a REIT focused on experiential properties. It is a triple-net lease REIT with over 352 properties and over 200 tenants across the U.S. and Canada focused on "experiential properties." Its sprawling $6.9B portfolio includes cinemas, fitness destinations, ski resorts, zoos, amusement parks, concert stadiums, casinos, museums, sports complexes, private schools, and early childhood education centers. From various Topgolf and AMC Theatres to iFLY indoor skydiving simulators, EPR's diverse portfolio has allowed it to capture entertainment-seeking consumers of all ages and interests.

EPR continues its strategy of diversifying away from movie theaters and investing more heavily in experiential properties like health and wellness retreats to kickstart future growth, such as climbing gyms, hot springs, and Miraval spa hotels, as key expansion areas.

EPR Stock Quant Breakdown

As tariff uncertainty continues to batter markets, EPR's 8.1% dividend yield and ability to provide steady income and potentially hedge against inflation make it the most attractive REITs on the market today. With two years of consecutive dividend growth, it's improving balance sheet should hopefully increase its dividend consistency score over time.

EPR Dividend Scorecard (as of 4/9/25)

EPR's upward trajectory is reflected by three-year AFFO growth of 14%, a 204% difference from the 4% sector median, as well as three-year dividend growth (CAGR) of 14.7%, a 297.7% difference from the sector median.

Like the other four other REITs mentioned in this piece, EPR provides both a way to hedge against tariff uncertainty and a source of steady income.

Conclusion

Tariffs have unnerved Wall Street, but there's no need to panic. Utilizing my tried and true barbell approach of seeking out stocks with strong fundamentals and more dependable, income-generating stocks such as the five REITs in this article will help diversify your portfolio and protect your existing investments. Feel free to explore SA Quants other Top REITs.

We have many stocks with strong buy recommendations, and you can filter them using stock screens to suit your specific investment objectives. Consider using Seeking Alpha's 'Ratings Screener' tool to help find stocks that achieve diversification into desired sectors you like. Alternatively, if you're looking for a select number of Quant Strong Buy recommendations on a monthly basis, you might want to explore Alpha Picks.

data.title: Bonds First Quarter - A Tale Of Two Markets And Tariffs
data.description:
By John R. Mousseau, CFA

The first quarter of 2025 saw yields on US Treasuries (Tsy) rise at the start of the year with the anticipation of Donald Trump’s inauguration and the expectation for greater spending and for higher potential inflation pushing yields higher in early January. The remainder of the quarter and the first part of April saw Treasury yields come down on the back of weaker economic news and then a large drop in yields in early April on the tariffs that the administration revealed last week.

After the initial rise in yields to start the year, economic numbers supporting a slowing economy began to take hold. This is a partial list: more-tame inflation numbers, higher levels of credit card delinquencies, much lower quits rates on jobs, slowing JOLTS numbers (job offerings), various Federal Reserve district manufacturing as well as services indices slowing, a slowdown in both existing as well as new-home sales. Though initial jobless claims were well-behaved, continuing claims have remained stuck to the upside. And now that DOGE layoffs in the Federal government have begun, the market is anticipating those workers being added to the jobless rolls as well as the unemployment rise (which has started).

Muni bond yields were not reacting to economic news as much as to political news. After beginning the year with muted supply (as usually happens), tax-exempt supply began to build towards the latter part of the quarter. But the real thorn for munis was the chatter in Washington about the possible taxation of municipal bonds as a revenue-raising measure. This talk clearly hurt the muni market, and you can see it in the absolute yields but more importantly in the yield ratio numbers in the chart above.

My colleague Ben Pease has laid out the various possibilities being discussed, which include taxation as well as a possible 28% cap on the benefit of federal tax exemption. See “Municipal Bond Tax Exemption”. Our base case is that in the end, Congress will leave the municipal bond alone. Part of that case involves looking at the federal agency reductions and the fact that state and local governments may be picking up activities currently done at the federal level. It would be disingenuous on the part of Congress to tell municipal governments to pick up these bills and then to take away their ability to finance activities at an advantageous rate.

The tariff tornado of this past week sent Treasury yields quickly lower as the carnage in equity markets drove investors to the safety of Treasuries – as usually happens in time of crisis. Munis also picked up the pace – in part because they had become cheap. And with tariffs dominating the headlines and equities sinking, the muni taxation issue has moved to the back burner. However, volatility in markets at the start of this week has propelled both Treasury and muni yields higher.

Below is a chart showing the change since April 4th.

Maturity
